coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and covalent organic frameworks based FRET immunosensor for sensitive detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Sunli
Liang, Nini
Hu, Xuetao
Li, Wenting
Guo, Ziang
Zhang, Xinai
Huang, Xiaowei
Li, Zhihua
Zou, Xiaobo
Shi, Jiyong
coredata.dc:description: The combination of carbon dots (CDs) with covalent organic frameworks (COFs) was used to design an innovative sensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) in food samples. Carbon dots were used as fluorescence donors, covalent organic frameworks as fluorescence acceptors. The antibody (Ab) specific to E. coli O157:H7 was used to form a CD-Ab-COF immunosensor by linking CDs and COFs. The antibody was specifically bound with E. coli O157:H7, which caused the connection between CDs and COFs to be interrupted, and the carbon dots exhibited fluorescence restoration. The sensor exhibited a linear detection range spanning from 0 to 106 CFU/mL, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 7 CFU/mL. The analytical performance of the developed immunosensor was evaluated using spiked food samples with different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7, validating the capability of assessing risks in food testing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Escherichia coli O157:H7
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Carbon dots
Covalent organic frameworks
Immunosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Bio-conjugation of CDs with e. Coli O157:H7 antibody
Working principle of FRET-based fluorescent immunosensor
Evaluation of the selectivity of fluorescent probes
Analytical performance assessment of the immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220058); Foundation of Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor (202074); Innovation of Agricultural Science and Technology of Jiangsu Province (CX(21)2016); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD); National Natural Science Foundation of China (32272407); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20200103, BK20220111); Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System (CARS-27); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022313).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of carbon dots, (b) UV absorption spectrum of CDs, (c) XRD of CDs, (d) 3D fluorescence spectrogram of CDs, (e) FTIR of CDs, (f) XPS full spectrum scanning of CDs.
(a) SEM image of COFs, (b) TEM image of COFs, (c) XRD of COFs, (d) 3D fluorescence spectrogram of COFs, (e) FTIR and (d) XPS full spectrum of COFs.

(a) FTIR comparison of CDs with and without antibody linkage, (b) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of CDs with and without antibody linkage, (c) Variation in the zeta potential of CDs as a result of antibody attachment, (d) Quenching of CDs by variation concentrations of COFs, (e) Fluorescence change of carbon dots at different action times of carbon dots and COFs, (f) SEM image of CD-Ab-COF.
Changes in fluorescence intensity of the sensor in L. monocytogenes, Salmonella, S. aureus, and E. coli O157:H7.
(a) Reaction curve of 0–106 CFU/mL E. coli O157:H7 with CD-Ab-COF, (b) Linear standard curve, (c) TEM image of E. coli O157:H7 interacting with CD-Ab-COF.
Detection results and recoveries of E. coli O157:H7 in milk.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and covalent organic frameworks based FRET immunosensor for sensitive detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab, School of Food and Biological Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212013, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Agricultural Product Processing and Storage Lab
School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of carbon dots (CDs) with covalent organic frameworks (COFs) was used to design an innovative sensor based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) for the detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) in food samples. Carbon dots were used as fluorescence donors, covalent organic frameworks as fluorescence acceptors. The antibody (Ab) specific to E. coli O157:H7 was used to form a CD-Ab-COF immunosensor by linking CDs and COFs. The antibody was specifically bound with E. coli O157:H7, which caused the connection between CDs and COFs to be interrupted, and the carbon dots exhibited fluorescence restoration. The sensor exhibited a linear detection range spanning from 0 to 106 CFU/mL, with the limit of detection (LOD) of 7 CFU/mL. The analytical performance of the developed immunosensor was evaluated using spiked food samples with different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7, validating the capability of assessing risks in food testing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a normal resident bacterium in the intestinal tract, which exists in large quantities in the intestinal tract of constant temperature animals and can be widely distributed in nature after being excreted in feces (Rompré, Servais, Baudart, de-Roubin, & Laurent, 2002). In most cases, E. coli and the host are in asymbiotic relationship that isbeneficialto both, but some E. coli are pathogenic. In recent years, there has been an increasing concern regarding the impact of E. coli on human life and health, particularly due to its potential contamination of food, drinking water, and air. (Zheng et al., 2023). Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E.coli O157: H7) is a highly pathogenic pathogen and a subgroup of Enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli (EHEC) (Song et al., 2023), as well as a group of E. coli capable of producing Shiga toxin (STEC) (Choi et al., 2022). It has become a daily concern for global food safety. E. coli O157:H7 infects the body through the contaminated food, which leads to serious symptoms such as hemorrhagic enteritis (Lin, Wang, Li, & Cui, 2019), renal failure, even being life-threatening (Han, Huang, Liao, Li, Lyu, & Ma, 2020). To reduce these risks, it is essential to diagnosis bacterial contamination rapidly and accurately (Chen et al., 2023). In addition to the conventional approaches employed for E. coli detection, electrochemical biosensors (Shi et al., 2022) and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (T. Li et al., 2020) were developed in recent years. However, the operations of these methods for E. coli O157:H7 were complicated and time-consuming. Biosensing technology based on Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) may be a viable detection method (Kim et al., 2020). FRET presents notable advantages including cost-effectiveness, ease of operation, and applicability for rapid measurements of a limited number of samples, even in complex media or macromolecular complexes (Liang et al., 2023).

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer is a tool to identify changes related to molecular recognition behavior (Liu, Feng, Qian, Wang, & Zhu, 2019) and has been used to detect subtle relationships between acceptors and donors at distances of less than 10 nm (X. Yu, Zhang, Zhang, Xue, Li, & Wu, 2018). This process involves a non-radiative transfer phenomenon. It occurs when the excited fluorophore of the donor molecule transfers its energy resonantly to the ground state acceptor molecule through long-range dipole–dipole interactions (Bu, Zhuang, Yang, & Ping, 2014). Usually, fluorescent proteins and organic dyes were chosen as fluorescent materials for FRET. However, several drawbacks such as low stability, a short duration of observation and photobleaching inhibited their application.

As a type of fluorescent material that has gained significant prominence, carbon dots (CDs) are structurally diverse with a wide range of potential structures and many unique properties (Kang & Lee, 2019; Manzoor et al., 2023). Fluorescent carbon dots have garnered significant interest due to their superior attributes such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, stable fluorescence, as well as notable electron-donating and electron-absorbing capabilities. These characteristics have propelled fluorescent carbon dots as a preferred choice over conventional fluorescent dyes and carbon quantum dots (D. Zhang, Jiang, Zhao, Dong, Feng, & Chen, 2019). CDs can be easily conjugated with target molecules for detection purposes. In addition, CD starting materials are available in large quantities, enabling low cost and mass production during synthesis. Its application in FRET-based biosensing is important.

Luminous covalent organic frameworks (COFs) consist of covalently bonded organic molecules (Xia et al., 2023). They exhibit better stability than metal organic frameworks (MOFs) (M. Li et al., 2023). They have been widely applied in identification, gas storage, separation and catalysis owing to the advantages of porosity, low density and high crystallinity (Gong, Guan, & Jiang, 2023). Exploring COFs as fluorescent sensors is still in its infancy (Y. Yu, Jin, Ren, Wang, Zhu, & Wang, 2023), but COFs have the unique capability to offer sites with electron-donating properties for specific molecules or ions. According to the fundamental mechanism of electron migration, these COFs may display distinctive fluorescence characteristics and can be used as chemical sensors to identify and discover multiple analytes (N. Zhang, Wei, Ma, Tian, & Wang, 2021). Several mechanisms of fluorescence have been detected in COFs-based sensors (Skorjanc, Shetty, & Valant, 2021), one of which can be used as a sensor for amines, enabling energy transfer by forming hydrogen bonds with the hydrogen or nitrogen atoms of the amine groups (Tang, Huang, Peng, Chang, Li, & Zhong, 2020). This sensor can transfer energy to COFs via antibody using the FRET approach. The validation of the sensor for amines provided some theoretical basis for the success of this experiment.

In this essay, a FRET-based immunosensor was employed for the detection of E. coli O157: H7 by E. coli O157:H7 antibody (Ab). The antibody was used to modify CDs as a fluorescent molecule, while COFs were used as quenchers. CD-Ab-COF fluorescent biosensor was constructed using the bioconjugation of CDs to antibody (CD-Abs) and their interaction with COFs (CD-Ab-COF). The exclusive affinity of E. coli O157:H7 to the antibody interrupted the energy transfer from the CDs to the COFs, resulting in a change in the fluorescence of CDs, which was used for the swift identification of E. coli O157:H7.

l-ascorbic acid, ethanol, acetic acid, n-butanol, 1,3,5-tris (4-aminophenyl) benzene, 1,4-dioxane, acetone, 2,5-dimethoxybenzene-1,4-dicarboxaldehyde, methanol, tetrahydrofuran, 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), sodium chloride (NaCl), Sodium phosphate dibasic (Na2HPO4), Monosodium phosphate (NaH2PO4) and potassium bromide (KBr) were purchased from MACKLIN (Shanghai, China). Luria-Bertani (LB) broth sourced from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Suzhou, China). The above reagents were all analytical grade supplies and had not been further purified. E. coli O157: H7 antibody and 20 × Tris Buffered Saline (TBS buffer) were purchased from Bioss Biologicals Ltd (Beijing, China). E. coli O157:H7 standard bacteria were ordered from Shanghai Shifeng Biotechnology Company (Shanghai, China). Milk samples bought from Jiangsu University Kaiyuan Supermarket (Zhenjiang, China). Nutrient agar media were purchased from Data Lab Supplies (Hunan, China).

A one-step synthesis method was employed for the preparation of CDs (B. Zhang, Liu, & Liu, 2010). 1.1 g of ascorbic acid was added to 25 ml of deionized water, and then the solution was stirred on a magnetic stirrer. 25 ml of ethanol was incorporated into the solution while stirring until the solution was clear and transparent. The homogeneous solution was placed inside a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to thermal treatment at 180 °C for a duration of 8 h. The solution underwent a transformation, resulting in a dark brown color. The presence of the dark brown solution indicated the successful formation of the CDs. Subsequently, this solution was subjected to dialysis for a period of 24 h using cellulose ester dialysis membranes (MW: 10000). The dialyzed solution was maintained at 4 ℃ in a refrigerator for subsequent analysis.

In order to synthesize covalent organic frameworks (COFs), the method described in the literature was improved (Xu, Gao, & Jiang, 2015). 10.54 mg of 1,3,5-tris(4-aminophenyl) benzene and 8.73 mg of 2,5-dimethoxybenzene-1,4-dicarboxaldehyde were added to 4.5 ml solution including 2 ml of 1,4-dioxane, 2 ml of butanol and 0.5 ml of methanol. After sonicating for 20 min to obtain a thoroughly mixed solution, acetic acid (12 M, 0.050 ml) was incrementally added drop by drop and reacted for 2 h at room temperature. Subsequently, 0.45 ml of 12 M acetic acid was added to the above solution and reacted in a water bath at 70 °C for 24 h. After heating, the solution was allowed to cool down to the temperature of the surroundings and washed with tetrahydrofuran and acetone respectively for 3 times, dried under vacuum and stored at a temperature of 4 °C in a refrigerator for future utilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-conjugation of CDs with e. Coli O157:H7 antibody
ce:section.ce:para: Using E. coli O157:H7 specific antibody, the EDC/NHS method allowed for coupling to CDs. E. coli O157:H7 antibody stock solution was diluted with 20 × TBS buffer at a volume ratio of 1:5000. Firstly, 400 mM of EDC and 100 mM of NHS were added to 20 ml of CDs respectively. The mixture was subjected to stirring at normal room conditions for a duration of 30 min to activate the carboxyl groups (–COOH) on the surface of CDs. Then 1000 µl of E. coli O157:H7 antibody was added in a drip-wise manner, and the solution was subjected to stirring for a duration of 2 h under dark conditions. Finally, the solution was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C overnight to facilitate the thorough covalent attachment of antibody to CDs, ensuring optimal conjugation.

E. coli O157:H7 was put into LB broth medium and incubated in shaking bed at 37 ℃ for 12 h. E. coli O157:H7 was subjected to a sequential 10-fold dilution series with 0.9 % NaCl solution. 20 μl of various concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 dilutions were taken and spread on the sterile agar nutrient medium, incubated inverted at 37 ℃ for 12 h. The number of colonies obtained on the petri dishes was counted and the various dilutions of E. coli O157:H7 with different concentrations were stored in the refrigerator at 4 ℃.

One-step synthesis of the CDs used ascorbic acid as a substrate. As observed in Transmission Electron Micrograph (TEM) of Fig. 1 a, CDs were uniformly granular with a particle diameter of 8 nm-10 nm. UV spectrum of CDs exhibited a prominent absorption peak at 305 nm (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the π → π* transition occurring in the CO bonds. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) of CDs has a broad peak between 20° and 40° with a peak at 30° (Fig. 1c). The optimal emission wavelength of CDs was detected using fluorescence spectrophotometer. Using three-dimensional wavelength scanning (Fig. 1d) showed that CDs demonstrated an optimal emission wavelength falling within the range of 410 nm to 430 nm. And it showed blue light under 365 nm UV light. The chemical structures of CDs were studied using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) and the result was shown in Fig. 1e. The FTIR analysis revealed a distinct peak at 1066 cm−1 which was attributed to CC bonds. The observed peak at 1404 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of COC. The peak at 1715 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bonds. The FTIR peak between 3064 cm−1 and 2809 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibration of OH. Obviously, the hydroxyl groups on the CDs'surface were partially oxidized and converted to the carboxylate groups, and these results aligned with the previously reported literature (B. Zhang et al., 2010). The production of carboxylate groups on the surface of CDs allowed the amine groups on the antibody to react with them and thus link them together. The hydrophilic groups on the surface of CDs kept them stable in aqueous solutions and provided a medium for their hydrophilic reactions to proceed to subsequent reactions. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the elemental composition and chemical states of the CDs. In the full spectrum scan (Fig. 1f), the XPS spectra of the CDs exhibited distinctive peaks at 284.28 eV and 523.4 eV, which were attributed to the C1s and O1s, respectively. To further understand the chemical composition, the elements were fitted with peak splitting. The high-resolution XPS of C1s could be fitted with four peaks, 284.6 eV, 286.4 eV, 286.8 eV and 288.6 eV (Fig. S1a), which were corresponded to the CC, CO, CO and OCO, respectively (Greczynski & Hultman, 2020).Two peaks in O1s high-resolution spectra (Fig. S1b) were located at 532.65 eV and 533.98 eV and belonged to CO and O-CO (Edwards, Oishi, & Botelho, 2018). The above experimental results indicated that carbon dots were successfully synthesized. By measuring the total number of carbon dots produced (Fig. S2), the Photoluminescence Quantum Yield (PLQY) was calculated to be 50.8 %.

Hydrothermal synthesis was employed to synthesize COFs. The morphology of COFs could be clearly seen under Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) (Fig. 2 a) and TEM (Fig. 2b), which had a spherical shape with a relatively smooth surface. The particle sizes of COFs were ranged from 1.277 μm to 1.529 μm in SEM image, with a higher number between 1.4 μm and 1.5 μm (Fig. S3). The particle size of COFs measured by TEM image (Fig. S4) was 1.319 μm, which was similar to the results obtained by SEM, and these COFs particle sizes were around 1 µm. In the XRD spectrum of COFs (Fig. 2c), it was observed that the material had a total of four peaks at 5.6°, 7.42°, 9.70° and 25.5°, which corresponded to the four surfaces of (200), (210), (220), and (001), respectively (Xu et al., 2015). Using three-dimensional wavelength scanning (Fig. 2d) showed the maximum excitation wavelength of 420 nm. COFs showed yellow light under 365 nm UV light. Using FTIR to characterize COFs (Fig. 2e), the result showed the characteristic absorption peak of the benzene ring was between 1520 cm-1and 1430 cm−1 and the CO characteristic absorption peak was at 1680 cm−1 (Lyu, Diercks, Zhu, & Yaghi, 2019). The peak spanning from 1650 cm−1 and 1539 cm−1 was composed of imine (CN) bending vibrations and resonance vibrations, and the presence of this bond implied the successful synthesis of COFs (Gong et al., 2023; Su et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2015). In the full scan XPS spectrum of COFs (Fig. 2f), the peaks at 283.59 eV, 397.67 eV and 532.24 eV were corresponded to C1s, N1s and O1s. The various elements were separated into peaks to capture the chemical composition of the COFs. The XPS spectrum C1s could be fitted to 283.5 eV, 284.7 eV and 286.5 eV (Fig. S4a), which were attributed to CO, C6H6 and CO (Edwards et al., 2018; Solomon, Madix, & Stöhr, 1991). Fitting of peaks to N1s (Fig. S4b), the peak at 339.4 eV was corresponded to CN bonds (Nowicki, Pietrzak, & Wachowska, 2010), confirming the successful synthesis of COFs again. The O1s spectrum exhibited two distinct peaks positioned at 532.6 eV and 534.4 eV, indicating the presence of CO bonds (Fig. S4c).

During the antibody conjugation process, the coupling of antibodies often involves the utilization of two reagents, EDC and NHS, which act as coupling agents for efficient covalent linkage. In the presence of EDC and NHS, activation of the carboxylic acid groups was performed on the surface of CDs. Observation of CDs before ligation and CDs after antibody ligation by FTIR reveals that the peak at 1715 cm−1 has almost disappeared and a distinct spectral feature emerged at 1631 cm−1, indicating that an amide has been formed between the antibody and the carbon dots after ligation. The presence of CO bonds in the amide was responsible for the peak observed at 1631 cm−1 (Fig. 3 a). Due to the ability of the carboxylate groups at CDs to interact with the amine groups on the antibody, a new peak appeared between 3100 cm−1 and 3690 cm−1 (Chattopadhyay, Choudhary, & Singh, 2022), indicating that the antibody was successfully attached to CDs (Fig. 3a). As indicated in Fig. 3b, the fluorescence intensity decreased after the carbon dots were bio-conjugated with the antibody. The change in zeta potential of CDs before and after attachment of the antibody was compared. The average value of the potential of CDs was −37.3 mV when it was not attached, and changed to −18.5 mV after attachment of the antibody (Fig. 3c). The above experiments showed that the antibody was successfully attached to CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of FRET-based fluorescent immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the fluorescent immunosensor was constructed using two different dyes, CDs as the fluorescent group and COFs as the quenching agent. The carboxylate groups on CDs underwent an amidation reaction with the amino groups on the antibody. This condition allowed the antibody to attach to CDs and formed CD-Abs complex. The imine (CN) in the synthesized COFs material was essential for maximum interaction and biological coupling with CD-Abs. During the detection process, CD-Abs were attached to the COFs surface through association the CN on the COFs surface with the sulfhydryl (-SH) of the antibody to form COF-SH and the hydrogen bond between the amine group of the antibody and the COFs (Merí-Bofí et al., 2017; Tang et al., 2020). During the process of detection, CD-Abs attached to COFs surface by certain stacking action and by the interaction between the imine of COFs surface and the sulfhydryl of the antibody when there was no bacterial sample to be tested. Through these interactions, CDs were able to get closer to the COFs surface, thus allowing the energy of CDs to be transferred to COFs via FRET, and eventually CDs were able to undergo substantial fluorescence quenching. However, When the tested bacteria were present, the antibody had high specificity with E. coli O157:H7. Antibody and bacteria formed an immune complex. Affinity of antibody towards its antigen led to a break in the connection between CDs and COFs, and CDs moved away from the surface of COFs, which eventually interrupted the energy transfer from the donor (CDs) to the acceptor (COFs). The fluorescence of CDs was restored.

CD-Abs fluoresced blue when irradiated with 365 nm monochromatic light. To investigate the changes in CDs resulting from different concentrations of COFs, 1 ml of phosphate buffered saline (PBS, A mixture formed by mixing 19 ml of 0.1 mol/L sodium dihydrogen phosphate and 81 ml of 0.1 mol/L disodium hydrogen phosphate, pH 7.4) was prepared. Then, the buffer solution was supplemented with 500 μl of CD-Abs and 500 μl of COFs. After the addition of different concentrations of COFs, the energy transfer from CD-Abs to COFs was explored through the fluorescence burst of CDs. Using fluorescence spectrophotometer, the changes in fluorescence values of different concentrations of COFs after binding to CD-Ab and the changes in fluorescence values of CD-AB-COF sensor at different reaction times were collected at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, respectively. According to the findings depicted in Fig. 3d, the fluorescence signal of CDs experienced significant quenching upon the addition of various concentrations of COFs (0.1–0.9 mg/ml). The fluorescence of CDs decreased or even disappeared as the concentration increased. If the COFs concentration was too high, its own color masked the fluorescence intensity of CDs. On the other hand, too low concentrations of COFs did not have a satisfactory effect on the reduction in CD fluorescence intensity. In order to be able to observe the subsequent fluorescence changes, the optimal quenching concentration was chosen to be 0.4 mg/ml. In order to select the optimal reaction time, fluorescence measurements were taken every ten minutes after the formation of the CD-Abs-COF complex (Fig. 3e), and it was observed that between 10 min and 30 min, the energy of CDs was transferred to COFs, giving rise to a decrease in its own fluorescence intensity, and the fluorescence quenching effect was almost unchanged at 30 min and 40 min, while the fluorescence intensity at CDs started to recover after 40 min, so a 30-minute incubation period was deemed optimal..

The surface morphology of COFs and CD-Abs conjugate after interaction was investigated to verify the morphology of CD-Ab-COF complex. From the SEM image in Fig. 3f, it was observed that when CD-Abs successfully adsorbed onto the COFs surface, the shape of COFs changed from spherical to irregular with numerous protrusions. The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs prior to and following the addition of COFs remained almost unchanged, and the fluorescence lifetimes of CDs almost overlapped with those of CD-Ab-COF (Fig. S5). Calculations based on Table S1 indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of CDs exhibited a marginal shift, with a decrease from 4.27 ns to 4.11 ns, upon the addition of COFs. Thereby, τ0/τ = 1, where τ0 and τ were the lifetimes of CDs under quencher-free and quencher-loaded conditions, respectively. Hence, it was inferred that the quenching of effect of COFs on CDs was of static quenching (Zu et al., 2017). FRET occurred between CD-Abs and COFs as evidenced by changes in their fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the selectivity of fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella, Staphylococcus aureus, and Escherichia coli O157:H7, commonly present in milk samples, were chosen to assess the specificity of the fluorescent probes. Bacteria at the same concentration (105 CFU/mL) were combined with the CD-Ab-COF sensor, followed by a 30-minute incubation period. Subsequently, the fluorescence of the incubated samples was measured to compare their fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4 ). Analysis of the CDs peak at 442 nm revealed that the fluorescence intensity of the other three bacteria was nearly identical to that of the blank sample. However, the fluorescence intensity detected after the addition of E. coli O157:H7 was higher than that of the other three bacteria. Thus, this fluorescence sensor can be effectively utilized for the selective detection of E. coli O157:H7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance assessment of the immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli O157:H7 was selected as a typical pathogenic microorganism to assess the efficacy of the developed CD-Ab-COF FRET immunosensor for quantitative analysis of intact bacterial cells. As discussed about the principle of fluorescence immunosensor sensor, the interaction between COFs and CDs resulted in diminishment of fluorescence intensity. To evaluate the quantitative analysis capabilities of this technique, a serial dilution of E. coli O157:H7 cells ranging from 0-106 CFU/mL was performed. After optimizing the conditions, CD-Abs were mixed with 0.4 mg/ml COFs in an equal volume (500 μl) in PBS and reacted for 30 min. Then 1 ml of E. coli O157:H7 with different concentration gradients was added to the prepared seven groups of CD-Ab-COF immunosensors respectively. The mixture was reacted for 30 min so that E. coli O157: H7 could fully bind with the antibody. Utilizing 365 nm light for excitation during detection, the fluorescence recovery results at CDs were observed (Fig. 5 a) and the relevant dose response curves was plotted (Fig. 5b). As could be observed in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 442 nm exhibited a positive correlation with the amount of E. coli O157:H7 present, and the fluorescence was mainly due to the specific affinity and binding of E. coli O157:H7 to the antibody. The disruption of the CD-Ab-COF complex linkage due to specific binding resulted in the progressive restoration of the fluorescence signal at CDs. The TEM image of the mixture in Fig. 5c further confirmed that the formation of CD-Ab-E. coli O157:H7 assembly was a condition for the resurgence of fluorescence intensity. There was a linear correlation between the increase in fluorescence intensity at 442 nm and the logarithm of the bacterial cell count. (Fig. 5b). The change in fluorescence intensity at 442 nm was plotted as a function of the recovered fluorescence intensity and the logarithm of the number of bacterial cells as a vertical coordinate and the logarithm of the number of bacterial cells was plotted as a horizontal coordinate, and the linear equation was obtained as y = 6.7151x + 639.18767, suggesting a close relationship with a coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.97727. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 7 CFU/mL. The detection method employed in this study was compared with other methods documented in the literature (Table S2). The analysis revealed that the developed sensor exhibited exceptional sensitivity and accuracy. This approach enabled the swift and sensitive detection of E. coli O157:H7. The FRET immunosensor that was developed demonstrated high specificity and selectivity towards the target pathogen, along with good reproducibility.

E. coli O157:H7 was taken under consideration as one of the most hazardous foodborne microorganisms and can be transmitted through contaminated food and water (Mozaffari et al., 2022). Milk was easy to be contaminated because of its rich nutrition (Mu et al., 2022). To assess the performance of the proposed FRET immunosensor in real-world scenarios with potential interfering factors, milk samples were selected as the test matrix and diluted with PBS. The prepared milk samples were contaminated with 1 ml of E. coli O157:H7 at concentrations that spanned from 0 to 104 CFU/mL. After thorough mixing, 1 ml of milk containing E. coli O157:H7 was taken and reacted with CD-Ab-COF in PBS for 30 min and then tested. Fluorescence values corresponding to concentrations ranging from 0 to 104 CFU/mL were input into the regression equation, resulting in precise detection values of 9.693, 102.329, 1098.194, and 8709.636. The recoveries of E. coli O157:H7 at different spiked concentrations (0–104 CFU/mL) range from 87.10 % to 109.82 % (Table 1 ). This result indicated the developed fluorescent sensor has potential value for the identification of E. coli in food and other biotic samples.

A high-sensitivity immunosensor based on FRET technology was developed for precise quantification of intact E. coli O157:H7 cells. This immunosensor employed carbon dots and covalent organic frameworks. CDs reacted with amine groups on antibody via carboxylate groups, thus linking together to form CD-Abs. CD-Abs could be adsorbed on the surface of COFs due to interactions between imines and sulfhydryl groups and the hydrogen bond between the amine group of the antibody and COFs. These interactions kept the fluorescent indicator CDs in close proximity to the COFs, resulting in a substantial energy transfer from CDs to COFs via Abs employing FRET and a decreased fluorescence capacity of CDs. However, under the influence of E. coli O157:H7, antibody recognized the target with high specificity. Antibody bound to antigens in the extracellular plasma of the pathogen to form immune complex. In addition, the affinity of the antibody for their antigens led to weakened binding to the COFs, thus interrupting the energy transferred between CDs and COFs, which resulted in the recovery of fluorescence at CDs. The LOD for this method was 7 CFU/mL. Using this method, detection of E. coli O157:H7 was faster and more sensitive than before and avoided the time-consuming and complicated drawbacks of previous experimental procedures. In addition, since different antibodies were specific for pathogens, the sensor could be used to detect specific strains using specific antibodies.

Sunli Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Nini Liang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xuetao Hu: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Wenting Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Ziang Guo: Software, Visualization. Xinai Zhang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Xiaowei Huang: Investigation, Validation. Zhihua Li: Software, Visualization. Xiaobo Zou: Supervision. Jiyong Shi: Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20220058); Foundation of Jiangsu Specially-Appointed Professor (202074); Innovation of Agricultural Science and Technology of Jiangsu Province (CX(21)2016); Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD); National Natural Science Foundation of China (32272407); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20200103, BK20220111); Earmarked Fund for China Agriculture Research System (CARS-27); Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BE2022313).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138663.
